artists.name: Steve Winterburn
artists.bio: Steve Winterburn works primarily in bronze and clay, producing figurative and animal sculptures of rare anatomical precision. Based in London, his work moves between classical tradition and inventive anthropomorphic compositions — subjects caught at the exact threshold between human and animal. His bronzes carry a textural richness that rewards close looking.

artists.name: Andrian Melka
artists.bio: Andrian Melka is a London-based sculptor working across bronze, clay, marble and stone. His portraits and figurative work are defined by an unflinching anatomical rigour — faces and bodies rendered with a psychological weight that goes beyond likeness. Trained in classical traditions, Melka's practice is a sustained argument for the enduring relevance of the human form.

artists.name: Adam Matano
artists.bio: Adam Matano sculpts in bronze and clay, occupying a rare zone where anatomical precision meets playful anthropomorphism. His animal and human figures share a visual language — the same muscular intelligence applied to both, as if the boundary between species is mostly a matter of posture. Based in the United States, his work is collected internationally and recognised for the wit embedded in its technical mastery.

artists.name: Pierre-Jean Chabert
artists.bio: Pierre-Jean Chabert works in bronze and clay from his Paris studio, producing animal and figurative sculptures distinguished by a deconstructed realism — forms that are simultaneously precise and deliberately unresolved. His textured surfaces carry the trace of process: the hand, the tool, the decision to leave something incomplete. The result is work that feels caught mid-breath.

artists.name: Pati Guidi
artists.bio: Pati Guidi is a São Paulo-based ceramicist and sculptor whose work draws on the figurative tradition while pushing it toward the ornamental and the visceral. Working in ceramics, clay and experimental materials, her pieces move between classical anatomy and expressive distortion — the human body as a site of ritual, beauty, and controlled excess.

artists.name: Andrew Joseph Keith
artists.bio: Andrew Joseph Keith works in clay and stone, producing figurative and human-form sculptures that push the body toward geometry. His work is defined by dynamic, tension-filled poses and a surface discipline that sits between realism and structural abstraction — figures that look as though they are in the process of becoming something else.

artists.name: Sasha Samsonova
artists.bio: Sasha Samsonova works exclusively in ceramics, producing abstract and deconstructed forms that resist easy categorisation. Her pieces occupy the threshold between pottery and sculpture — vessels that have abandoned function, objects that propose new ones. The surfaces carry a deliberate rawness, a refusal of finish that gives her work an unresolved, searching quality.

artists.name: Yoshitoshi Kanemaki
artists.bio: Yoshitoshi Kanemaki carves directly into wood, producing multi-faced figurative sculptures of extraordinary conceptual density. His figures — often a single body splitting into several faces — explore identity, perception, and the impossibility of singular selfhood. Working from Tokyo, his practice draws from Japanese craft tradition while arriving at something entirely contemporary and unsettling.

artists.name: Kenzo Dodo
artists.bio: Kenzo Dodo works from Osaka, producing figurative sculpture that layers classical composition with a quiet, distinctly Japanese sense of restraint. His pieces often focus on the human body at rest — stillness as a form of intensity. The surfaces are worked to a fine grain, the gestures deliberate and economical, the emotional register somewhere between serenity and melancholy.

artists.name: Centina Alexa
artists.bio: Centina Alexa is a sculptor whose practice explores organic form and the human figure with an expressive, material-forward approach. Her work draws from the body's relationship to nature — forms that appear grown rather than made, surfaces that carry the memory of touch.

artists.name: Jason Tarpley
artists.bio: Jason Tarpley's sculpture operates at the intersection of folk art and dark fantasy — rough-hewn, strange, and deeply committed to its own logic. His work pulls from Americana, mythology, and outsider art traditions to produce figures that feel ancient and invented simultaneously. Unsettling without being gratuitous, singular without being obscure.

artists.name: Ukokobi Osaro Osemengbe
artists.bio: Ukokobi Osaro Osemengbe is a Lagos-based sculptor whose work engages with the human figure through an Afro-contemporary lens — drawing from Igbo visual traditions, urban Nigerian life, and a formal sophistication that sits comfortably in dialogue with the global sculpture canon. His pieces are politically present without being didactic, personal without being insular.

artists.name: Thomas Hildenbrand
artists.bio: Thomas Hildenbrand is a German sculptor working in the classical tradition with a contemporary eye. His figurative work in stone and bronze carries the weight of Central European craftsmanship — technically assured, compositionally restrained, and quietly monumental. His studio practice is a patient, deliberate negotiation with material and form.

artists.name: Sahar Motaharinezad
artists.bio: Sahar Motaharinezad is an Iranian sculptor working with ceramic and mixed materials, exploring the female form and identity through a practice shaped by Persian artistic heritage and contemporary feminist discourse. Her work navigates intimacy and resistance, producing pieces that are fragile in material and forceful in intention.

artists.name: Kiki Smith
artists.bio: Kiki Smith makes figurative sculpture, printmaking, and drawing that centres on the body—its vulnerabilities, spirituality, and relationship to the natural world. Working in glass, bronze, paper, and wax, she draws on medieval imagery, fairy tales, and feminist discourse to create works of visceral intimacy.

artworks.title: Lure
artworks.material: Bronze with string, forged bronze hook, and pewter sinker

artists.name: Thomas Houseago
artists.bio: Thomas Houseago makes large-scale figurative sculpture in plaster, bronze, and corten steel, channelling the expressionist legacies of Picasso, Giacometti, and tribal art into a raw, physically confrontational idiom. His towering figures and fractured heads command architectural space with psychological weight.

artists.name: Ibrahim Mahama
artists.bio: Ibrahim Mahama transforms discarded jute sacks—used to transport cocoa across West Africa—into vast sculptural installations that shroud public buildings and institutions. The accumulated labour and trade histories embedded in the material make visible the weight of postcolonial economic systems.

artists.name: Do Ho Suh
artists.bio: Do Ho Suh creates translucent fabric replicas of domestic spaces—apartments, corridors, stairwells—that can be collapsed, shipped, and re-erected anywhere. The works interrogate the relationship between inhabitation and memory, displacement and belonging, personal history and collective architecture.

artists.name: Arlene Shechet
artists.bio: Arlene Shechet builds ceramic sculpture that resists tidiness—forms tilt, sag, and balance on invented plinths, as though caught mid-thought. Her practice in porcelain, stoneware, and bronze fuses historical craft traditions of Delft and Japanese ceramics with an improvisational, gestural sensibility.

artists.name: Roxy Paine
artists.bio: Roxy Paine is best known for his Dendroid series—monumental stainless-steel trees that replicate the branching logic of organic growth through industrial fabrication. He also builds SCUMAK machines that autonomously extrude polyethylene blobs, exploring the intersection of natural systems and technological process.

artists.name: Huma Bhabha
artists.bio: Huma Bhabha constructs imposing figures from cork, clay, wire, and found objects, their surfaces scarred with Cycladic, African, and South Asian formal echoes. Her totemic works carry a persistent sense of trauma and resilience, standing at the convergence of ancient sculpture and postcolonial anxiety.

artworks.title: Bleekmen
artworks.material: Clay, wood, wire, Styrofoam, plastic, cast iron, fabric, aluminum, synthetic pol

artworks.title: There Is No Killing What Can't Be Killed
artworks.material: Cork, styrofoam, synthetic polymer paint, and oil stick

artists.name: Ursula von Rydingsvard
artists.bio: Ursula von Rydingsvard carves cedar beams into monumental forms that evoke bowls, pods, and landscapes at architectural scale. Dusted with graphite to deepen their dark, furrowed surfaces, her sculptures carry the memory of displacement and rural labour across generations.

artists.name: Mark Manders
artists.bio: Mark Manders stages his entire practice as an ongoing Self-Portrait as a Building—a fictional architecture populated by painted bronze figures, incomplete sentences, and arrested gestures. His uncanny tableaux fuse Surrealist logic with the quiet tension of a room mid-evacuation.

artworks.title: Room with Chairs and Factory
artworks.material: Wood, iron, rubber, painted epoxy, painted ceramic, painted canvas, painted wig,

artworks.title: Working Table
artworks.material: Painted epoxy, painted wood, painted canvas, iron, and offset print on paper

artists.name: Nairy Baghramian
artists.bio: Nairy Baghramian makes elegantly discomfited sculpture—cast aluminium sections in flesh-like tones that slump against walls or sprawl across floors, suggesting prosthetics, furniture, or body parts on the verge of collapse. Her work questions the display conventions that normally uphold and frame objects.

artworks.title: Maintainers A
artworks.material: Pigmented wax over styrofoam, cast aluminum, and painted aluminum clamp with cor

artworks.title: Reclining (Pauline)
artworks.material: Powder-coated cast aluminum, stainless steel, and bronze

artists.name: Alicja Kwade
artists.bio: Alicja Kwade manipulates everyday materials—clocks, stones, mirrors, wire—to probe time, value, and perception. Her spare, precise installations short-circuit familiar logic: a stone replaced by another of identical weight but opposite cost, a clock running backwards at the speed of the Earth's rotation.

artists.name: Theaster Gates
artists.bio: Theaster Gates works across ceramics, architecture, and social practice, using salvaged fire hoses, wooden beams, and slip-cast vessels as material carriers of Black American cultural history. His practice is inseparable from community infrastructure: he rebuilds neglected buildings on the South Side of Chicago as cultural venues.

artists.name: Bharti Kher
artists.bio: Bharti Kher uses the bindi—a mass-produced cosmetic dot worn on the forehead—as both material and symbol, applying thousands of them to fibreglass animals, mirrors, and canvases to question beauty, ritual, gender, and the layering of cultural identities. Her monumental animal sculptures carry quiet, loaded intensity.

artists.name: Petrit Halilaj
artists.bio: Petrit Halilaj makes sculpture and installation rooted in fragile personal and collective histories, from Kosovo's post-war rebuilding to queer intimacy under repression. His works—oversized insects, hand-embroidered stages, objects from his childhood bedroom—transform vulnerability into monumental tenderness.

artists.name: Carlos Bunga
artists.bio: Carlos Bunga constructs temporary architectural interventions from cardboard boxes and adhesive tape, transforming gallery spaces into labyrinthine enclosures he then methodically destroys. The cycle of building and unbuilding makes the impermanence of structures—domestic, institutional, political—visceral and visible.

artists.name: Mona Hatoum
artists.bio: Mona Hatoum transforms domestic and medical objects into instruments of unease—oversized kitchen graters, hospital beds laced with electric current, maps rendered in human hair. Her sculptures hold violence and tenderness in suspension, making the familiar feel precarious and the body perpetually under threat.

artworks.title: Pin Rug
artworks.material: Stainless steel pins, canvas, and glue

artworks.title: + and -
artworks.material: Sand, steel, aluminum, and electric motor

artists.name: Toshiko Takaezu
artists.bio: Toshiko Takaezu closed the openings of her ceramic vessels to create sealed forms that became repositories for sound—small clay balls inside producing a gentle rattle. Drawing on Zen philosophy and her Japanese-American heritage, her moon jars and cylindrical forms balance meditative stillness with expressive surface.

artists.name: Simone Leigh
artists.bio: Simone Leigh makes large-scale bronze and ceramic figures that are resolutely Black and female, fusing African architectural traditions—Bahian house-dresses, Dogon granary facades—with the American history of medicine's exploitation of Black women's bodies. Her 2022 Venice Biennale Golden Lion cemented her as a defining voice of contemporary sculpture.

artists.name: Leilah Babirye
artists.bio: Leilah Babirye carves wood and builds ceramics into defiant portrait-heads of LGBTQ+ refugees, drawing on Ugandan visual traditions to make visible lives erased by state violence. Her expressive, mask-like figures bristle with found materials—bottle caps, wire, beads—and carry the intensity of protest and mourning.

artists.name: Bosco Sodi
artists.bio: Bosco Sodi builds thick paintings and sculptural objects from pigment mixed with sawdust, earth, and cellulose paste that dry over months into cracked, geological surfaces. His work embraces accident and process: forms split and settle unpredictably, recording time and climate as material facts.
